title: Pus-Like Vulvar Discharge in Female Dogs: Causes and Urgency
meta_desc: If your female dog has pus-like discharge, it could be a life-threatening emergency. Learn about Pyometra and why immediate vet care is required.
content_html:
Understanding Vulvar Discharge in Female Dogs

Finding a thick, white, yellow, or bloody discharge from your dog's vulva is a sign that should never be ignored. This substance is usually pus, which indicates a significant bacterial infection. In unspayed female dogs, the most common and dangerous cause is a condition called Pyometra, which is an infection of the uterus. In spayed dogs, while less common, it could indicate a severe urinary tract infection, vaginitis, or a stump pyometra.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

Pus-like discharge is considered a high-urgency medical emergency. If your dog is not spayed, this is a life-threatening situation. Pyometra can cause the uterus to fill with toxins that leak into the bloodstream, leading to sepsis, organ failure, or a ruptured uterus. Even if your dog is still eating or acting normally, the presence of pus suggests an active infection that requires immediate veterinary intervention to prevent a rapid decline in health.

How a Photo Can Help the Triage Process

When you call or head to the emergency vet, having a clear photograph of the discharge can be incredibly helpful. Capturing the color, consistency, and amount of the fluid helps the veterinary team triage your pet more effectively. A photo provides a clear visual record of the symptoms before your dog potentially grooms herself clean, allowing the doctor to make faster decisions regarding diagnostic tests like blood work, X-rays, or an ultrasound.

faqs.question: Can I wait a few days to see if the discharge clears up?
faqs.answer: No. Especially in intact females, waiting even a few hours can be the difference between a successful surgery and a fatal outcome. Always seek emergency care for pus-like discharge.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms of Pyometra to watch for?
faqs.answer: Common signs include increased thirst (polydipsia), frequent urination, lethargy, loss of appetite, vomiting, and a swollen or painful abdomen.

faqs.question: Will my dog need surgery?
faqs.answer: If a Pyometra is confirmed, the standard of care is an emergency spay to remove the infected uterus. Your vet will also likely prescribe aggressive fluid therapy and antibiotics.
